skill.name: subagent-driven-development
skill.description: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
skill.summary: Orchestrate implementation tasks using fresh subagents with two-stage review: spec compliance then code quality
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 38 static findings are false positives. The skill is a meta-skill containing prompt templates only - no executable code, scripts, network calls, or file operations. Static scanner misinterpreted GraphViz DOT syntax as shell commands, YAML 'risk: unknown' as cryptographic weakness, and standard code review language as reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: GraphViz DOT Syntax Misidentified as Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged DOT graph syntax (digraph, -> arrows) in SKILL.md as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are GraphViz diagram definitions for workflow visualization, not actual shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Verified by reading actual file content - lines contain DOT graph syntax for workflow diagrams

dangerous_patterns.title: YAML Frontmatter Misidentified as Cryptographic Weakness
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 'risk: unknown' in YAML frontmatter as weak cryptographic algorithm. This is metadata describing risk level, not cryptographic code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: YAML frontmatter at top of SKILL.md contains skill metadata, not cryptographic algorithms

dangerous_patterns.title: Git SHA References Misidentified as Cryptographic Issues
dangerous_patterns.description: Mentions of 'SHA' (git commit SHAs) flagged as cryptographic weakness. These are version control references in the example workflow, not cryptographic implementations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Git SHA references in documentation describe commit tracking, not cryptographic algorithms

dangerous_patterns.title: Example File Path Misidentified as File System Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged '~/.config/superpowers/hooks/' in example workflow as hidden file access. This is documentation showing an example path, not actual file operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Example path shown in user dialogue, not code that accesses the filesystem

dangerous_patterns.title: Code Review Language Misidentified as Reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: Phrases like 'Read the implementation code', 'verify by reading code' flagged as system/network reconnaissance. These describe standard code review and QA workflows, not malicious scanning.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Prompt templates instruct subagents to review code as part of development workflow - legitimate software engineering practice

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Template Structure Misidentified as External Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: YAML/JSON-like structures in prompt templates flagged as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are Task tool configurations for dispatching subagents, not actual shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Task tool YAML configurations define how to invoke subagents - documented Claude Code functionality

content.value_statement: This skill automates implementation task execution by dispatching fresh subagents for each task, with automatic two-stage review (spec compliance then code quality) ensuring high-quality results without manual oversight.
actual_capabilities: Dispatch independent subagents for each implementation task in a plan
Execute two-stage review: spec compliance first, then code quality
Maintain session context while avoiding subagent context pollution
Automate review loops until implementer fixes all issues
Track task progress using TodoWrite and mark completion after approvals
limitations: Only works within same session (cannot dispatch to parallel sessions)
Requires pre-existing implementation plan with clear, independent tasks
Tasks must be loosely coupled (tightly coupled tasks cause conflicts)
More subagent invocations mean higher API usage compared to manual execution

use_cases.title: Feature Implementation with Quality Gates
use_cases.description: A development team lead uses this skill to execute a multi-task feature plan. Each task gets implemented by a fresh subagent, reviewed for spec compliance, then checked for code quality before moving to the next task.
use_cases.target_user: Development team leads and senior developers

use_cases.title: Automated Refactoring Projects
use_cases.description: A developer needs to refactor a large codebase across multiple files. They create a plan with independent refactoring tasks. The skill executes each task automatically with built-in quality verification.

use_cases.title: Batch Implementation of Related Changes
use_cases.description: A DevOps engineer uses this skill to implement a series of infrastructure changes from a plan. Each change goes through spec review and code quality checks automatically.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Implementation Dispatch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the implementer-prompt.md template to dispatch a subagent for the first task in my implementation plan. The task is: [paste task description here].
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting to execute an implementation plan with the first task

prompt_templates.title: Spec Compliance Review Request
prompt_templates.prompt: After the implementer completes Task N, dispatch a spec reviewer using spec-reviewer-prompt.md to verify the implementation matches the requirements. Task requirements: [paste requirements]. Implementer report: [paste report].
prompt_templates.scenario: Verifying that implemented code matches specification

prompt_templates.title: Code Quality Gate Check
prompt_templates.prompt: After spec compliance passes, dispatch a code quality reviewer using code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md. Include the git base SHA and head SHA for the task. Review: [describe what was implemented].
prompt_templates.scenario: Ensuring implementation meets code quality standards

prompt_templates.title: Full Workflow Execution
prompt_templates.prompt: Execute my implementation plan using subagent-driven-development. Read the plan file at [path], extract all tasks, and process each one through implementer, spec reviewer, and code quality reviewer before marking complete.

output_examples.input: Execute this implementation plan for adding user authentication
output_examples.output:
Task 1: Implement login endpoint
- Subagent implements, tests, commits
- Spec reviewer: All requirements met
- Code quality reviewer: Approved

Task 2: Add password reset flow
[continues with same pattern...]

output_examples.input: What does this skill do differently from manual execution?
output_examples.output: Unlike manual execution, subagent-driven-development: (1) Uses fresh subagent per task to avoid context pollution, (2) Runs automatic two-stage review after each task, (3) Stays in same session for continuous progress, (4) Catches issues early before they compound.

output_examples.input: Why use two-stage review (spec then quality)?
output_examples.output: Spec compliance review first ensures only requested features are built. Code quality review second ensures the implementation is well-built. This order prevents wasted effort on high-quality code that doesn't match requirements.

best_practices: Provide complete task context upfront - subagent should not need to read the plan file
Always answer subagent questions before letting them proceed with implementation
Never skip review loops - if reviewer finds issues, implementer must fix and re-review
Dispatch tasks sequentially, not in parallel, to avoid conflicts
anti_patterns: Starting code quality review before spec compliance is verified (wrong order)
Skipping spec compliance review and proceeding directly to code quality
Accepting 'close enough' from spec compliance - requirements must match exactly
Letting implementer skip self-review before reporting back

faq.question: How is this different from the executing-plans skill?
faq.answer: executing-plans dispatches to a parallel session. subagent-driven-development stays in the same session, uses fresh subagents per task, and adds two-stage review after each task.

faq.question: Why use fresh subagent per task instead of one subagent for all tasks?
faq.answer: Fresh subagent avoids context pollution. Each task is independent with clear boundaries. A single subagent might carry forward assumptions or confusion from previous tasks.

faq.question: What happens if a subagent asks questions during implementation?
faq.answer: Answer questions clearly and completely before the subagent proceeds. Do not rush them into implementation. Clarifying upfront prevents rework later.

faq.question: Can I skip the code quality review if spec compliance passes?
faq.answer: No. Both reviews are required. Spec compliance ensures you built what was requested. Code quality ensures it was built well. Skipping either defeats the purpose of the workflow.

faq.question: What if the reviewer finds issues but the implementer disagrees?
faq.answer: The reviewer is authoritative on compliance and quality standards. If implementer disagrees, they should discuss with the reviewer and reach consensus. Do not proceed with unresolved issues.

faq.question: How do I handle tightly coupled tasks in my plan?
faq.answer: This skill requires independent tasks. If tasks are tightly coupled, refactor the plan first to separate them, or use a different workflow skill like executing-plans.
